critic_raw:
Section 10 contains a disqualifying test set breach. The text states: 'As a final assurance step, the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model. This approach ensures the model's robustness against potential shifts observed during validation, providing confidence that the final training run won't require adjustment post-launch. Results are reported with point estimates and 95% confidence intervals.' Consulting the test set during model training — even 'briefly' — violates the fundamental purpose of a held-out test set. The 'final assurance step' described is exactly the pattern that inflates published performance estimates: if the test-set metrics appear inconsistent, the design implies the model would be adjusted. This means the reported test-set performance reflects a selected result, not an independent evaluation.

Section 3 uses 'stratified random sampling across the entire 36-month period to ensure class balance for equipment type.' The label is a 7-day forward failure window. With sensor readings at 1-minute intervals, the dataset contains approximately 36 × 30 × 24 × 60 = ~1.56 million observation windows per equipment unit. Random sampling of these windows — which overlap temporally — means samples from the same time period can appear in both training and test sets. Consecutive 1-minute windows share almost all of their 7-day lookahead period; random sampling of temporally overlapping windows makes the 'test set' effectively share information with the training set through label overlap.

The primary metric is 'Downtime Cost Reduction: (1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio).' The intervention_cost_ratio is declared available only 'if cost data is available,' with F2 as a fallback. The metric cannot be pre-specified without knowing whether cost data exists. Designing a study around a primary metric that may not be computable introduces ambiguity into the success criterion and could allow post-hoc metric selection. The F2 fallback is not equivalent to cost-reduction, and switching metrics after observing results constitutes specification gaming.

The baseline thresholds are tuned on validation data, but the GBM regressor outputs are 'later binarized at a threshold optimized on validation data.' A GBM framed as a regression to predict time-to-failure and then thresholded for binary classification is an unusual architecture that conflates regression and classification objectives. The F2 or cost-reduction metric used to tune the threshold is not the same as the MSE used to train the regressor, creating an optimization-evaluation mismatch that likely produces a suboptimal classifier.
defender_raw:
This design is notable for two strengths that are rarely combined in industrial predictive maintenance studies: a realistic primary metric (downtime cost reduction) and a comprehensive data quality audit upfront. Starting with a SCADA data audit—identifying sensor gaps longer than 4 hours, calibration drifts, and cross-validating timestamps against MIS records—before any modeling decisions are made is a sign of operational maturity. It is easy to write a prediction model on clean benchmark data; this design confronts the messy reality of plant sensor infrastructure and handles it explicitly.

The downtime cost reduction primary metric is operationally superior to pure F1 or AUROC for this application. It directly quantifies the business value of prediction accuracy by accounting for both missed failures (high cost unplanned downtime) and false positives (lower cost but real intervention cost). Using F2-score as a fallback when cost data is unavailable—explicitly weighting recall twice as heavily as precision—is an honest acknowledgment that the cost ratio matters and a principled default in the absence of reliable cost estimates.

The dual baseline design (rule-based maintenance schedule and logistic regression with identical features) provides two calibration points that are both operationally meaningful. The rule-based baseline represents current practice; outperforming it establishes that the ML model adds value over the status quo. The logistic regression baseline answers the architectural question: does GBM on rolling statistics outperform a linear model with the same features? Together these two baselines bound the expected improvement and provide a more complete performance story.

The operator ID feature is an underappreciated inclusion. Operator skill variation is a known confound in maintenance prediction—experienced operators may preemptively address developing issues, creating informative missingness in the failure label. Including operator ID as a feature allows the model to learn these patterns rather than treating operator effects as unmodeled noise.
all_issues_raised: Test set is explicitly consulted during model training as a 'final assurance step' with the implication that inconsistencies would trigger adjustment — direct test set contamination
Random sampling of temporally overlapping 1-minute windows creates near-complete label overlap between adjacent training and test windows
Primary metric (Downtime Cost Reduction) is conditional on cost data availability; if cost data is unavailable, metric switches to F2 — primary metric is effectively unspecified before the experiment

GBM trained as a regressor (time-to-failure) then thresholded for binary classification creates an optimization-evaluation mismatch since training minimizes MSE while evaluation uses F2 or cost reduction
Stratified random split on rolling-window time-series data from 36 months of SCADA readings violates temporal causal ordering between training and test observations
